blocks.text: Chapter 7.
Doing, Waiting, and Overplanning
The ideas in 30 seconds
We are wired to act even when doing nothing is statistically superior, and to excuse harmful inaction because it feels less culpable than harmful action.
Willpower is a poor scheduling tool: externally imposed deadlines reliably beat self-set ones, which in turn beat no deadlines at all.
Every plan is systematically overoptimistic; the only reliable fix is Kahneman's "outside view" — pricing your project against the actual track record of similar projects.
We irrationally overpay to eliminate the last sliver of risk while ignoring far cheaper reductions of large risks — even though zero risk almost never exists.
Extra information, missing alternatives, the pull of "right now," and the daily news all quietly degrade decisions without adding knowledge.
Doing Something Beats Doing the Right Thing — In Appearance Only

Action bias is the compulsion to act in unclear situations, even when action makes things worse. The cleanest demonstration comes from Israeli researcher Michael Bar-Eli, who analyzed hundreds of soccer penalty kicks. Shots are distributed roughly evenly — a third to the left, a third to the center, a third to the right — so a goalkeeper's best statistical play is to stay put. Yet keepers dive left or right on the overwhelming majority of kicks. Why? A keeper who dives and misses looks heroic; one who stands still while the ball flies past looks like a fool.

The same logic drives investors to churn portfolios, doctors to over-treat ambiguous cases, and executives to launch initiatives just to appear decisive. Warren Buffett compressed the antidote into one line: "We don't get paid for activity, just for being right."

Dobelli traces the bias to our evolutionary past: in a hunter-gatherer world, sprinting at the first flicker of a shadow beat contemplation — thinking hard while a lion charged got you removed from the gene pool. Modern life reverses the payoff. Most contemporary problems reward patient analysis over reflexive motion, but our applause still goes to the doers. Society compounds the error by honoring frantic activity and treating deliberate waiting as weakness, so we act not because it helps but because inaction is unbearable — and unrewarded.
The rule: when a situation is unclear, resist the itch. If acting confers no genuine advantage, sitting on your hands is the sophisticated move, however uncomfortable it feels.
Inaction Harms Too — It Just Doesn't Feel Like It

Omission bias is action bias's mirror image: when both acting and not acting cause harm, we judge the passive harm as more acceptable. Dobelli's illustration uses vaccination arithmetic. Suppose a disease will kill 10 out of every 1,000 children, while the vaccine that prevents it kills 5 in 1,000. Rationally you vaccinate — it halves the death rate — yet many parents refuse, because a child killed by their active choice feels catastrophically worse than a child killed by the disease they merely failed to prevent.

The bias runs through law and medicine. A doctor who withholds treatment from a dying patient is treated far more leniently than one who actively ends the same life, though the outcome is identical. Failing to declare income feels less criminal than forging a tax document. Building nothing while a housing crisis worsens attracts less blame than building something that fails.

Omission bias is harder to spot than action bias precisely because it hides in what we don't do. The corrective is symmetry: judge your inactions by the same standard as your actions. "I didn't do anything" is not an alibi when doing something would have prevented harm.

Notice the pairing: action bias makes us do too much when situations are new and unclear; omission bias makes us do too little when both paths cause damage. Clear thinking means asking, in every case, which error you're currently primed for — and correcting against that one.
Deadlines Beat Willpower: The Science of Procrastination

Procrastination — postponing unpleasant but important work in favor of immediate comfort — is irrational because we know the delay hurts us, yet we delay anyway. The gap between intention and action is powered by the fact that hard work costs now while its payoff arrives later.

Behavioral economist Dan Ariely ran the definitive test with his MIT students, who had to submit three papers per semester under three different regimes. One class got evenly spaced, professor-imposed deadlines. A second class could set its own binding deadlines in advance. A third had only one deadline: everything due the last day of term. The results ranked cleanly — the class with dictated, evenly spaced deadlines earned the best grades; the self-set-deadline class came second; the total-freedom class did worst. Freedom to schedule yourself is an invitation to postpone.

Two lessons follow. First, self-control is not a fixed trait but a depletable resource — it drains through the day and needs recharging, which is why resolving to "just try harder" fails. Second, structure outperforms resolve. The students who set their own deadlines did better than the free class only because they converted intention into external commitment.

Dobelli's practical program: eliminate distractions before you start (he wrote his novels offline, with the internet physically unavailable), break work into chunks with fixed dates, and — most powerfully — make your deadlines public. Telling others when you'll deliver converts a private preference into a social contract, importing the discipline of Ariely's best-performing class into your own life.
Everything Takes Longer and Costs More: The Planning Fallacy

The planning fallacy, named by Daniel Kahneman, is our systematic tendency to underestimate the time, cost, and risk of our projects while overestimating their benefits — even when we know that past projects overran. It is overconfidence applied to schedules and budgets, and no amount of experience seems to cure it: people who have blown every previous deadline still believe the next plan.

The monument to the fallacy is the Sydney Opera House. Planned in 1957 for completion in 1963 at a cost of $7 million, it opened ten years late, in 1973, at $102 million — roughly fourteen times budget — and only after the design was scaled back. Daily life reproduces the pattern in miniature: the to-do list you write each morning assumes a distraction-free fantasy day, so most items roll over to tomorrow.

Why does it persist? We plan from the inside, focusing on our specific project and imagining the best-case sequence of steps, while ignoring outside influences — illness, supplier delays, scope creep — that don't appear in the plan but always appear in reality. Wishful thinking does the rest: we want the project to be fast and cheap, so we forecast that it will be.

Kahneman's remedy is the outside view: ignore your inside story and consult the base rate — how long did projects like this actually take, whatever their planners intended? If similar kitchen renovations averaged nine months, your estimate is nine months, not the four your contractor promises. For high-stakes plans, add a premortem: gather the team, assume the project has failed spectacularly, and have everyone write the story of how. The imagined autopsy surfaces the risks the rosy plan suppressed.
Paying Any Price for Zero: The Zero-Risk Bias

Harvard economist Richard Zeckhauser exposed zero-risk bias with a Russian roulette thought experiment: people will pay far more to remove the last bullet from the revolver (reducing bullets from one to zero) than to remove one of four bullets — even though each removal cuts the probability of death by the same amount. Complete elimination of a risk feels categorically different from mere reduction, so we buy it at wildly inflated prices.

The pattern generalizes: cutting a risk from 5% to 0% feels far more valuable than cutting it from 55% to 50%, though the improvement is identical. The U.S. Delaney Clause of 1958 enshrined the bias in law, demanding a total ban on carcinogenic additives in food — a zero-risk standard that consumed resources which, spent on bigger hazards, would have saved many more lives.

The uncomfortable truth is that zero risk barely exists. Money in a savings account carries bank risk and inflation risk; crossing the street carries mortal risk; even state guarantees depend on the state's solvency. The only rational strategy is to accept that you live with residual risk and spend your risk-reduction budget where it buys the most safety per dollar — usually on trimming large risks, not on exterminating tiny ones. Chasing the seductive round number of zero is how individuals and governments overpay for feelings of safety while leaving real dangers untouched.
Four Quick Traps: Information, Alternatives, Now, and News

Information bias is the delusion that more data always means better decisions. Doctors order diagnostic tests whose results won't change the treatment; analysts gather forecasts that sharpen their confidence but not their accuracy. Extra information costs time and money and often just fertilizes overconfidence — ask, before gathering it, whether it could actually change what you'd do.

Alternative blindness means comparing an option only against the status quo instead of against the next-best alternative. A student weighs "MBA versus no MBA" and ignores what else two years and the tuition could buy; an investor compares a bond to cash rather than to every other available asset. Buffett's discipline applies: the true yardstick for any choice is the best alternative you're forgoing.

Hyperbolic discounting is our inconsistent impatience: offered $1,000 today or $1,100 in a month, most people grab the $1,000 — yet offered the same pair twelve months out, they happily wait the extra month. The nearer a reward, the more the "now" intoxicates us, which is why we sign up for gyms in January and eat cake in the moment. The closer temptation sits, the less you should trust your in-the-moment self.

Dobelli closes his catalog with the news illusion: daily news is engineered to be novel and dramatic, not relevant, and it is to the mind what sugar is to the body. Of the thousands of items you consumed last year, try naming one that led to a single better decision. His prescription is radical abstinence — skip the news entirely and spend the reclaimed attention on long-form journalism and books, which deliver understanding rather than stimulation.
In practice
Before your next "do something" impulse in an ambiguous situation, write down what standing still would cost; act only if action has a demonstrable edge.
Audit one recent inaction (a treatment declined, a conversation avoided, an investment skipped) as harshly as you would judge a harmful action.
Break your current big task into chunks, attach dated deadlines, and announce them to a colleague or friend this week — imported accountability outperforms willpower.
For any project estimate, look up how long three comparable projects actually took and use that base rate; then run a ten-minute premortem: "It's a year from now and this failed — why?"
List your top five risks and reallocate effort from eliminating tiny risks entirely to shaving points off the biggest one.
Before ordering more research or data, ask: "Could the answer change my decision?" If not, decide now — and trade thirty minutes of daily news for a book chapter.
